title: Collections
summary: Use Map, Set, and typed arrays to store, organize, and efficiently retrieve data collections

sections.title: Maps and Sets Are Specialized Arrays
sections.content: If arrays are like numbered lockers, Maps are like labeled lockers where you choose the key (not just a number). A Map stores key-value pairs where keys can be any type — strings, numbers, even objects. This is perfect for dictionaries, caches, or any lookup table. A Set is like a bag that automatically rejects duplicates — add the same item twice and the second addition is ignored. Sets are ideal for tracking unique items like visited pages or tags on a post. Both are built into TypeScript and support generic type parameters to ensure type safety, like 'Map<string, number>' for a dictionary of names to scores.

sections.title: Tracking Product Inventory with a Map
sections.content: Uses a Map to store product names and quantities, then checks stock levels

sections.title: Sets for Uniqueness and Fast Lookups
sections.content: A Set automatically enforces uniqueness — adding a value that already exists simply does nothing. This makes Sets the right choice whenever you need to track 'what have I seen?' without caring about counts or order. Sets also provide O(1) lookup speed with .has(), which is much faster than checking an array with .includes() for large collections. The trade-off is that Sets do not store indexes — you cannot get 'the third element' from a Set. TypeScript's Set type is generic: 'Set<number>' for a set of numbers, 'Set<string>' for strings. Use Sets for tags, unique IDs, or any collection where duplicates are meaningless.

sections.title: Using an Array When a Set or Map Would Be Better
sections.content: The #1 intermediate mistake is using arrays for everything, even when you need uniqueness or key-based lookups. Wrong approach: 'const visitedPages: string[] = []; if (!visitedPages.includes(url)) { visitedPages.push(url); }' — O(n) check every time. Correct approach: 'const visitedPages: Set<string> = new Set(); visitedPages.add(url);' — O(1) and automatically deduplicates. Similarly, using two parallel arrays instead of a Map is a code smell. If you find yourself calling .find() or .filter() to locate an item by a property, you probably need a Map.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A website analytics system needs to track unique visitor IP addresses per day and also store each visitor's page view count. Use a Set for today's unique IPs (starts empty) and a Map<string, number> for tracking how many pages each IP has viewed. Write code to: (1) add IP '192.168.1.1' viewing 3 pages, (2) add '10.0.0.1' viewing 1 page, (3) add '192.168.1.1' again viewing 2 more pages, (4) print total unique IPs and each IP's total page views.
hints: How do you update a Map value when the key already exists — do you need to get the old value first?
What does Set.add() do if the value already exists in the set?
How do you iterate over a Map to print each key and value?
